speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so we're talking about--

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to tell you, it's got to do something with those cousins. I just want to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are we rolling? OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So I just want to say something that maybe has nothing to do with that, so personal for myself. While I was being non-Jewish-- Aryan, they called it-- I fell that you're so free. Nobody's on your back. You don't have to prove anything to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought, it's not a bad way to be. Maybe-- and another thing what I thought, I thought I was the only one. The few of us were the only ones that survived, because nobody else did. So I thought, maybe I should stay anonymous and be a Gentile. Who'd know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But when I met Jewish people, [LAUGHS] the whole thing just escaped from me. And I knew that once a Jew, always a Jew. You can't change your identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were comfortable with it at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Must have been a relief to be able to tell the truth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. A relief it was, of course. But I mean, I was comfortable. I knew that that's what I am, that's what I was, and that's what I'm going to be. I won't change it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, so with this-- with the cousins, when I met up with them, we-- everybody already plotted to go back into the American occupation, because lots of Jews had family. Canada, America, They wanted to go to them. They were already in touch with. I quite didn't remember. I knew that we have family too, in Canada, but I couldn't quite remember their names or their addresses or anything. But this one cousin, he was older. He was already in his 40s. And he remembered. He knew. And he wrote to them already. And they-- they said that whoever survived, they want to bring them to Canada. So we had to get out from Poland and into the German occupation. And that wasn't easy. We had to bribe the guards. We had to pay money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Into the American--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the American zone.

speaker: interviewer
text: --controlled area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We had to pay money. I didn't have, definitely, but they did, because they were in Russia, and they were smarter. They already-- they had money. They paid for me too, whatever. They, like, you paid for a head, ransom, sort of. And we-- you know, took a few months-- I don't know, maybe 2, 3 months-- and we had to smuggle the border from Poland to Czechoslovakia. We had to actually walk. And then from there, they had already set up places where you could go in and they would put you on a train, they'll feed you, and you know, like relief organizations there. And it could have been, you know, from Israel. I don't know who they were exactly, but I mean, there was something waiting for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were organizations that helped you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The only thing, we couldn't bring any pictures. So from all the German occupation-- because I did have, you know, somebody took a picture of you. You had a picture. I did have some pictures, but we had to burn everything. We couldn't bring a document. I would have had this document, too, but you couldn't bring nothing. You were Jews from Turkey, you couldn't speak to anybody, and that's the way they transported us to-- to Germany, to the German occupation. So we came there, and they put us--

speaker: interviewer
text: What place did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Traunstein, it's called.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a city. A small city, but a nice city in Bavaria. And they put us into DP camps, they were called-- Displaced Person. And-- and we used to get a lot of relief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in that DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every family or single had a little sort of room, little cubby hole, just enough to survive, to stay. But it wasn't that bad. The DP camps weren't bad. I mean, we were free, at least. We could go and come and-- and do whatever we wanted. And, of course, then, it started already with Israel, with all the news. We had papers, we had radio. It was a life. We were living already. We weren't like-- like living dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till I came to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: '49. I came to Canada in the fall, October of 1949, so about two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years. What did you do for two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I met my husband. I got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's from Przemysl, from Poland, too. And he went to concentration camp and all this stuff, too, and he was left alone. And I met him there, and we got to know each other. A lot of marriages just took place because, you know, you needed somebody. You were so alone that you really needed a close soul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rebuild the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. So you got married there, and?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a little boy. [LAUGHS] Richard was born. And when Richard was born, he was a very cute child. He really was. Now, you know him, you don't know how cute he used to be. I'll have to show you some pictures. But he was gorgeous. And-- and I loved him so much. All my love and all my soul went into this child. I remember I used to look at him and think to myself, oh, god, I do have somebody in this world. I'm not all alone anymore. And it was a lot, meant a  lot. And then we got out-- well, it took all this time to get the passports and all the things that you had to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were sponsored by the family in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Canada, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the family in Canada that sponsored you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the sponsor was not of the family. He was sort of related, but not quite of the family. His name was Harry Weiner, and he's deceased now. He had big holdings as a farmer, a big farmer. And at that time, they wanted only farm hands, because it was right after the war, and they needed people to work. So he could sponsor somebody, because my family, they were all city people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in Brooks, and that's how we got--

speaker: interviewer
text: Brooks, Alberta?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alberta. And that's how we came to Brooks, to his farm. And he was pretty nice. He taught us everything we knew, actually, about, you know, about life here. It was a shock, too. It was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: First thing he did, he took my husband out and taught him how to drive a truck. Then he taught him how to milk cows. And little by little, we stayed about, I think, a year and a half with Harry on the farm. And then we bought a little-- well, his farm was about a few miles out of the city, of the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we got into town, we got to know people, and we bought a little house. And this little house we bought for $50 down and $50 a month. And the irony of it was that the man that sold us this little house was a German-- ancestry. But he was already Canadian. And we bought this little house. And we-- well, I don't know if we have time to tell you all the story about this little house. There was a story, too. And we moved into this town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was Brooks?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Brooks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, fifties, beginning of '50, something like that. And in Brooks, there was a very nice family, the Wolfmans. You know Eddie Wolfman, his mother and father. They lived in Brooks. They owned a store in Brooks, a grocery store. And they were very nice to us. I felt like my mother just came out. She was older. And she was something like a [YIDDISH]. And, oh, we used to go there for holidays. They came to us, and we used to go there. It was very nice. And we stayed in this town till '62.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have more children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I had a daughter and another son. And then, you know, with all the children getting older, it was nice in that town. It was very nice to bring up little children there. We moved to Calgary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in '62. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do? What was your occupation, besides bringing up the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Brooks, we had a furniture store. In the beginning, we had an upholstery shop, and I used to help my husband sew a little bit, do whatever. And then we had the furniture store. I used to help out there a little, too. But when we came to Calgary, I really wanted to strike out on my own, and I-- I opened a store by myself, a ladies-wear store. That was in '68 already, quite a few years later. And this was a very good move for me. I really enjoyed doing it and being with it. And I love clothes. And-- and it was nice, so I did that. And I-- of course, in those days-- now the kids are growing up, and they're getting older, and they get to university. And I have lots of time on my hands, so I did-- I stayed with the store for about seven years. Then I had to move from one location to another, actually closer to my-- to where I lived. But I wanted a little time to myself. And I had a customer that really wanted to buy my store, so I thought, well, I can't refuse to make a little money. I'll sell it. If I want another store, I'll open myself another one. And I sold it, and I-- and then I took a few nice trips. I went to Israel for the first time. That was '72. And I, you know, did all kinds of different things. And then I opened, actually, another store. And I had it till I retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're retired now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Clara, it's over 50 years since you were liberated, since the war. You think it's possible for it to happen again, the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I hate to sound so gloomy, but I really feel that it can happen again. In the right place, with favorable conditions like they had then, it can happen again. Because I think that anti-Semitism will never be erased fully. It's hidden. It's camouflaged. So ugly heads can always rise, and I really think that it can happen. I hope never, never. But we don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your testimony with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. It was a pleasure to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you are afraid the Holocaust may happen again, and you wanted to add what you think will prevent it from happening again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, I couldn't say exactly what, but my own feeling is that Jews need a very strong state, which is Israel, of course. And they should invest there, they should go there and live there-- not just go for a 2-week holiday. And I think if we had this in the background, we could leave, maybe, all over the world, but just in case you need, we have something to fall back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother and my aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother's with the shorter hair.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, god, that must have been a very long time ago, before she was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before she was married. So when will it be approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the early century. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how did you get the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, an aunt in Canada had it, and she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was sent to the aunt before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was the organization that I belonged to, the [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were on a--

speaker: interviewer
text: Field trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on a trip. And there's the picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my god, a long time ago. I think in the book there is the year on it. I think it's '32.

speaker: interviewer
text: '32. And how did you get that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got this book. The people from our town in Israel, they made this book, and this was one of the pictures that got into it. Somebody had it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK, this is it. We are done.

speaker: interviewee
text: What about the one with my brother in the book?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh yeah, that's right. I didn't-- we have one more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we marked it. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Clara, who is in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my younger brother, and he's with his school chums.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: The one on the right, the first one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gershon.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this photo taken, approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How old do you think he was then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He must have been either in grade one or two, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got it out from-- from a book that was done in Israel. It's called [INAUDIBLE], which is our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, want to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? This is Richard, my oldest son, and Noah, my granddaughter, Richard's daughter, and Judy, his wife, and little Talia.

speaker: interviewee
text: You could say hi, bubby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, bubby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hi, sissy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter Natalie. She lives in Toronto. And this is my son and daughter-in-law Nancy and Ron.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: They live in Williams Lake, BC.
